displayTitle: Splitting the Signal: Two Peaks or One?

beats.narration: Detecting an echo is half the job. The other half: splitting close reflectors, and judging what a signal probably is.

payload.title: SPLIT THE SIGNAL
payload.sub: The instrument separates — the inspector interprets.

beats.narration: Axial resolution lives on pulse length. A short pulse keeps two close reflectors as two peaks. A long ringing pulse smears them into one hump.

payload.heading: Pulse length sets axial resolution
payload.note: SHORT PULSE → TWO PEAKS · LONG PULSE → ONE HUMP

beats.narration: What shortens a pulse? Higher frequency, and heavier damping: a backing block soaks up the ringing. Broadband resolves; narrowband penetrates with more sensitivity.
The operator half is discrimination: build expectation from reference blocks with known reflectors, the part's manufacturing and service history, and probable flaw types.

payload.heading: Build the expectation before you scan

beats.narration: Detection is probabilistic, so scan with overlap from multiple directions. Resolution is what the instrument separates. Discrimination is what you interpret. Sharpen both.

payload.sub: Resolution is the instrument. Discrimination is you.
